rationale: The citing paper uses Park and John (2014) as a key prior finding that brand/product use can improve performance in product-related domains via self-efficacy, and it summarizes that result explicitly in both the introduction and theory section. This prior work helps motivate the paper's central research gap: what happens to performance on product-unrelated tasks when product-related identity is activated. The citing paper then develops and tests a new mechanism and boundary (identity deactivation and self-concept clarity) while keeping the broader product-to-performance line of inquiry.
relation_description: Chung and Johar build on Park and John's evidence that product or brand use can facilitate related-task performance. They extend that line by examining ownership-based identity activation/deactivation and showing downstream impairment on tasks labeled as unrelated to the activated identity.
